episode_title: Briefing chat: Pokémon turns 30 — how Pikachu and pals inspired generations of researchers
summary: This Nature Podcast episode marks Pokémon's 30th anniversary (February 27, 1996) by exploring the intersection between Pokémon and scientific research. Hosts discuss how Pokémon inspired generations of researchers, particularly biologists and paleontologists, through its collecting mechanics that parallel taxonomy and natural history classification. The episode features expert Miriam Nadav discussing examples including a Field Museum exhibition linking Pokémon to fossils, phylogenetic analysis of Pokémon evolutionary relationships, and multiple instances of real species named after Pokémon characters.
science_topics: Pokémon phylogenetic analysis and evolutionary trees
Biological evolution vs. individual metamorphosis in Pokémon
Taxonomy and species classification inspired by Pokémon collecting mechanics
Paleontology - fossil discovery of baby marine turtles from dinosaur era (Colombia)
Bee biodiversity - polyester bees (Hymenoptera) and morphological/DNA sequencing identification
Species naming conventions and nomenclature in biology
Insect collecting and natural history collecting practices
claims_to_note: Phylogenetic analysis of 646 Pokémon using actual phylogenetic software produced 'realistic looking' evolutionary tree with water-origin (2012 study by Matt and colleagues) - verify publication and methodology
Analysis suggested lampreys and bony fishes were earliest Pokémon types to reach present state - this is speculation applied to fictional species
Pokémon transformation is closer to metamorphosis than biological evolution - correctly notes that real metamorphosis occurs over long timescales, unlike sudden Pokémon transformations
Eight new bee species identified through morphological examination and DNA sequencing in Chile - claimed to be named after Pokémon
Fossils from Colombia initially thought to be plants were identified as baby marine turtles in 2023
policy_relevance: Field Museum (Chicago) hosting first US exhibition linking Pokémon fossils to real paleontological specimens - indicates science institution engagement with popular culture
Pokémon Go mentioned as game played by researchers - relevant to science communication and public engagement
Centre for Biodiversity Genomics (Canada) - research institution conducting species discovery work
key_quotes: For many researchers, especially biologists, watching the TV series or playing the video game or training cards have been a big part of their childhood... And for many, Pokemon was also an inspiration to do the science that they do today because it sparked their curiosity and love to discover new species or name new species and work with animals.

The collecting element obviously essential to the games and the trading cards and the cartoons and the movies and all the rest of it... [has] parallels here with taxonomy, right, and classification and essentially organising things in the tree of life.

Biological evolution happens within populations rather than individuals and it also takes place over successive generations. Whereas in the Pokemon world, an individual Pokemon transforms suddenly and we see the changes at an individual level... the process could be closer to metamorphosis rather than evolution.

publications.title: In the Light of Evolution: Volume III: Two Centuries of Darwin
publications.description: Two Centuries of Darwin is the outgrowth of an Arthur M. Sackler Colloquium, sponsored by the National Academy of Sciences on January 16-17, 2009. In the chapters of this book, leading evolutionary biologists and science historians reflect on and commemorate the Darwinian Revolution. They canvass modern research approaches and current scientific thought on each of the three main categories of selection (natural, artificial, and sexual) that Darwin addressed during his career. Although Darwin's legacy is associated primarily with the illumination of natural selection in The Origin, he also contemplated and wrote extensively about what we now term artificial selection and sexual selection. In a concluding section of this book, several science historians comment on Darwin's seminal contributions. Two Centuries of Darwin is the third book of the In the Light of Evolution series. Each installment in the series explores evolutionary perspectives on a particular biological topic that is scien

publications.title: Evolution in Hawaii: A Supplement to 'Teaching About Evolution and the Nature of Science'
publications.description: As both individuals and societies, we are making decisions today that will have profound consequences for future generations. From preserving Earth's plants and animals to altering our use of fossil fuels, none of these decisions can be made wisely without a thorough understanding of life's history on our planet through biological evolution. Companion to the best selling title Teaching About Evolution and the Nature of Science, Evolution in Hawaii examines evolution and the nature of science by looking at a specific part of the world. Tracing the evolutionary pathways in Hawaii, we are able to draw powerful conclusions about evolution's occurrence, mechanisms, and courses. This practical book has been specifically designed to give teachers and their students an opportunity to gain a deeper understanding of evolution using exercises with real genetic data to explore and investigate speciation and the probable order in which speciation occurred based on the ages of the Hawaiian Islands.

publications.title: In the Light of Evolution: Volume IV: The Human Condition
publications.description: The Human Condition is a collection of papers by leading evolutionary biologists and philosophers of science that reflect on the Darwinian Revolution as it relates to the human condition at levels ranging from the molecular to the theological. The book focuses on understanding the evolutionary origin of humans and their biological and cultural traits. The Human Condition is organized into three parts: Human Phylogenetic History and the Paleontological Record; Structure and Function of the Human Genome; and Cultural Evolution and the Uniqueness of Being Human. This fourth volume from the In the Light of Evolution (ILE) series, based on a series of Arthur M. Sackler colloquia, was designed to promote the evolutionary sciences. Each volume explores evolutionary perspectives on a particular biological topic that is scientifically intriguing but also has special relevance to contemporary societal issues or challenges. publications.title: The Next Decade of Discovery in Solar and Space Physics: Exploring and Safeguarding Humanity's Home in Space
publications.description: Groundbreaking advances in solar and space physics have provided key insights into the dynamic physical processes on the Sun and its influence on Earth, the near-Earth space environment, other planets in our solar system, and beyond. As we look to the next decade, future discoveries in the field will expand our knowledge of the cosmos and better prepare us for the impact of space weather events on critical systems and humanity. The Next Decade of Discovery in Solar and Space Physics: Exploring and Safeguarding Humanity's Home in Space, a National Academies' decadal survey, presents a prioritized strategy for basic and applied research to advance scientific understanding of the heliosphere and the origins of space weather, the Sun's interactions with other bodies in the solar system, and the interplanetary and interstellar mediums.
